beats.prompt: Quick recall. From Combinatorics, match each number of coin flips to the count of distinct head/tail strings.

feedback.correct: n flips → 2ⁿ strings. And those 2ⁿ strings are exactly the 2ⁿ numbers a length-n bit string can name: 0 to 2ⁿ−1.
hints: Each flip doubles the number of possible strings.
Match: 1 flip→2, 2 flips→4, 3 flips→8, 10 flips→1024.

beats.prompt: You owe a worker 1 gram of gold a day for 7 days. You may cut your 7-gram gold bar as few times as possible. You must cover exactly 1, 2, 3, … 7 grams each morning — but you can give change and take pieces back. What is the fewest number of cuts needed?

6 cuts.note: Worth testing — you don't need a fresh piece every day. You can take change back, so you can reuse pieces. The answer is much fewer than 6.

3 cuts.note: Close in spirit, but 2 cuts already give you 3 pieces {1, 2, 4} — and that's enough to make every total from 1 to 7 by combining and swapping.

2 cuts.note: Exactly right. Two cuts produce pieces of 1, 2, and 4 grams — the first three powers of two. Every total from 1 to 7 is a unique subset of those three pieces.

beats.prompt: One idea before we go further.

interaction.title: A bit = one yes/no
interaction.body: Each bit position asks: 'Is this power of 2 in the sum?' A 1 means yes, a 0 means no. That is all binary is — a column of yes/no answers, one per power of 2.

feedback.correct: Keep this in mind: each bit owns exactly one power of 2, and it either is or isn't in the sum.
hints: A bit is a binary digit: 0 or 1.
Each position represents a power of 2: …8, 4, 2, 1 (right to left).
Read the card, then tap Continue.

beats.prompt: The gold rod: 2 cuts make 3 pieces that cover all daily totals 1–7. The pieces are powers of 2. What is the largest piece (in grams)?

feedback.correct: Exactly — the pieces are 1, 2, and 4 (the powers of two); the largest is 4.
hints: The three pieces are the first three powers of 2. Which powers of 2 sum to 7?
1 + 2 + 4 = 7. The three pieces are 1, 2, 4. The largest is 4.
The answer is 4.

beats.prompt: Toggle the bits to build the target number. Each bit position is a power of 2 — turning it on adds that power to the running sum. Try building 7, then 100.

feedback.correct: 7 = 4 + 2 + 1 = 111₂. Each power of 2 is either in the sum (1) or not (0). That pattern of 1s and 0s is the binary label.
hints: Start with the largest power of 2 that fits: for 7, that's 4.
7 − 4 = 3 → next power ≤ 3 is 2; 3 − 2 = 1 → last power is 1.
Bits at positions 4, 2, 1 → pattern 111.

beats.interviewNote: Interviewers love 'is x a power of 2?' / 'how many 1-bits?' — all of it rests on this: a number is its set of powers of two. Lesson 5 turns that into one-line tricks: isPowerOfTwo(x) = (x & (x−1)) === 0 checks exactly whether there is one 1-bit; popcount counts them all. The uniqueness of binary representation is why those tricks work.
beats.prompt: Toggle any bit and watch the value change by exactly that power. Notice: no two different patterns give the same number — the representation is unique.

feedback.correct: Toggling bit k changes the value by exactly 2ᵏ — up if you add it, down if you remove it. Every number has a unique bit pattern, so numbers and patterns are the same thing.
hints: Each bit position owns one power of 2. Toggling it adds or removes that exact power.
If two patterns gave the same number, their difference would be zero — but toggling any bit always changes the value.

beats.prompt: Write 100 in binary. Which powers of 2 sum to 100?

feedback.correct: 100 = 64 + 32 + 4 = 1100100₂. The 1s mark the bit positions for 64, 32, and 4; everything else is 0.
hints: Subtract the biggest power of 2 that fits: 64 ≤ 100, so bit 64 is 1. Remainder: 100 − 64 = 36.

beats.prompt: Held-out transfer. Write 43 in binary using the same method: subtract powers of 2 from largest to smallest.

hints: Biggest power of 2 ≤ 43 is 32. Remainder: 43 − 32 = 11.

beats.prompt: Prove you've got it. Write 1000 in binary — the Green Book headline number.

interaction.scenario: 1000 is a sum of distinct powers of 2. Find all of them and write the binary label: a 1 where each power is present, a 0 elsewhere.

hints: Biggest power of 2 ≤ 1000 is 512. Remainder: 1000 − 512 = 488.

feedback.correct: Every non-negative integer is a unique sum of distinct powers of 2. Writing which powers are present (1) or absent (0) gives its binary label. Those 1s and 0s are bits — and they are the number. Next: how many bits do you need to name N things?
hints: Any number = a unique subset of {1, 2, 4, 8, 16, …}.
Binary is just which powers of 2 are in that subset.
Next lesson: the information bound ⌈log₂N⌉.
